front: Novel: "manuscripts don't burn" — a professor says this after learning a writer destroyed his manuscript about Pontius Pilate
Novel: the chairman of the literary organization MASSOLIT is decapitated by a streetcar, as predicted by a visiting professor
Novel: a poet whose alias means "homeless" is sent to a lunatic asylum where he meets a writer; his only belonging is a greasy black cap with an embroidered "M"
Novel: a vodka-loving demonic black cat named Behemoth beheads Bengalsky and reattaches his head at the Variety Theater
Novel: one title character uses a wish to free Frieda from a curse of waking daily next to the handkerchief she used to smother her child
Novel: a midnight Walpurgis Night ball is held in Apartment 50, attended by various denizens of hell
Novel: in the novel-within-a-novel, the Roman governor suffers continual headaches and is served by the secret policeman Aphranius and centurion Mark the Rat-Killer
Novel: Behemoth burns down the MASSOLIT headquarters above Griboedov's restaurant
Novel: one title character flies naked over Moscow
Novel: published posthumously, 26 years after its author's death; the author fought Soviet censorship throughout his career
Novel: in The Day of the Oprichnik, Vladimir Sorokin inverts this novel's famous line, writing "books burn well. Manuscripts go like gunpowder"
Novel: Dr. Preobrazhensky transplants a human pituitary gland and testicles into a stray dog named Sharik, turning him into Polygraph Polygraphovich Sharikov
Novel: after transformation, the title character gets a job driving around in a van strangling stray cats
Novel: Preobrazhensky's name derives from the word "transformation"; the character is possibly based on surgeon Serge Voronoff
Novel: Dr. Bormenthal notes that the pituitary gives "not rejuvenation but total humanization" and "predetermines the human image"
Novel: after surgery, a dog-turned-human chooses the name "Poligraf" after seeing it in an Orthodox Christian calendar
Novel: a professor berates his creation for offering "advice on a cosmic scale and of quite cosmic stupidity, on the redistribution of wealth... and at the same time you eat toothpaste"
Novel: a satire on Communist theories of human perfectibility; depicts Soviet apartment life during the New Economic Policy era
Novel: a researcher notes that a subject can read after pronouncing the word "delicatessen" backwards
Novel: Professor Persikov discovers a red ray that causes rapid growth; Alexander Rokk accidentally creates giant snakes, ostriches, and crocodiles instead of chickens
Novel: a plague of giant creatures unleashed on Moscow is stopped only by a mysterious August freeze
Novel: follows the Turbin family — Alexei, Elena, and Nikolka — during the Ukrainian Civil War; adapted into the play The Days of the Turbins
work: The White Guard / The Days of the Turbins

front: Author: Stalin reportedly saw The Days of the Turbins 15-20 times; his love for the play may have protected this author from persecution
work: The White Guard / The Days of the Turbins

front: Play: ends with the sound of the "Internationale" from offstage after the German and White coalition under Hetman Skoropadsky is defeated by Petlyura's pro-Soviet Rada
back: The Days of the Turbins (Mikhail Bulgakov)
work: The White Guard / The Days of the Turbins

front: Play: fictionalizes the life of Moliere; analogizes Stalin to Louis XIV
Play: disastrous four-year rehearsal period caused a rift between its author and Konstantin Stanislavski
Author: the story "Morphine" features Dr. Polyakov, who hallucinates a woman flying and holding pitchforks, then shoots himself
Play: Efrossimov's camera protects those it photographs from a deadly cloud of radiation; set in a supermarket where every shopper has died frozen in their last act
Play: set in the year 2222 where faucets dispense grain alcohol; engineer Rein's faulty time machine accidentally brings Ivan the Terrible to the present
Author: satirized Stanislavski and the Moscow Arts Theatre in a novel variously published as Theatrical Novel or Black Snow
Author: Anna Akhmatova wrote an elegy in this author's memory
Author: subject to heavy Soviet censorship; became unable to publish in the 1930s
Author: John Hodge's 2012 Olivier Award-winning play Collaborators depicts this author's relationship with Stalin, in which the two end up swapping work
